content.problemStatement:
There are NNN tasks waiting in line to be executed. The execution time for the ithi^{th}ith task is AiA_iAi​ seconds.

Chef has **two processors** to execute these NNN tasks. Both these processors work simultaneously. Each processor executes the assigned tasks one by one.

Chef assigns a **prefix** of these tasks to the first processor and the remaining tasks to the second processor.

For example, if there are 333 tasks, Chef can do one of the following:


Assign no task to the first processor. This means, the second processor will execute tasks 1,21, 21,2 and 333.
Assign task 111 to the first processor. This means, the second processor will execute tasks 222 and 333.
Assign tasks 111 and 222 to the first processor. This means, the second processor will execute task 333.
Assign tasks 1,21, 21,2 and 333 to the first processor. Thus, second processor would execute no tasks.
Find the **minimum** time in which all the tasks can be executed.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the number of tasks waiting to be executed.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space separated positive integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the execution time for each task.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line, the minimum time in which all tasks can be executed.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of tasks. The second line contains N space‑separated positive integers A1, A2, …, AN denoting the execution time of each task in order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible time needed to finish all tasks when the first processor receives a prefix of the tasks and the second processor receives the remaining suffix.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: total = 9. Best split after first task gives times 4 and 5, so answer = 5.\nTest case 2: total = 6. Splitting after three tasks gives 3 and 3, answer = 3.\nTest case 3: only one task, both processors cannot share it, answer = 5.

solution.approach: For each test case compute the total sum S of all task times. While scanning the array keep a running prefix sum P (the time taken by the first processor if it receives the first i tasks). For each i (including i = 0 meaning no task for the first processor) the time needed for the whole system is max(P, S‑P). The answer is the minimum of this value over all i. This works because the second processor always receives the suffix, so its total time is S‑P. The optimal split is the prefix whose sum is closest to S/2.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of tasks in that test case. The total across all test cases is O(2·10^5).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input array.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().strip().split()))
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it)
        a = [next(it) for _ in range(N)]
        total = sum(a)
        pref = 0
        ans = total  # split before first task
        for val in a:
            pref += val
            cur = pref if pref > total - pref else total - pref
            if cur < ans:
                ans = cur
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
